skills.name: accessibility-foundations
skills.description: The baseline accessibility every interface must meet — semantic HTML, visible focus, labelled controls, sufficient contrast, keyboard operability, and adequate touch targets. Treat as a hard constraint, not an enhancement.

skills.name: color-system
skills.description: Build a semantic, token-based colour system that is accessible in light and dark themes, with a chosen neutral and an accent kept separate from status colours.

skills.name: dashboard-design
skills.description: Design dashboards that answer questions, not just display data — summary before detail, state encoded in form, a clear scan path, and progressive depth via drill-down.

skills.name: accessible-data-tables
skills.description: Present tabular data so it is scannable and operable — real table semantics, aligned columns, sensible density, sorting/filtering, and responsible responsive behaviour.

skills.name: decision-engine
skills.description: The contextual design decision procedure — turn a project context into ranked, explained design recommendations with reasoning, confidence, tradeoffs, and alternatives. Run before implementing any interface.

skills.name: review-engine
skills.description: The structured design review procedure — score an implemented interface across UX, UI, accessibility, responsive, performance, and product dimensions, then drive the critique loop until quality holds. Run after implementing any interface.

skills.name: system-generator
skills.description: The design system generation procedure — derive tokens (primitive → semantic → component), type scale, spacing, and component rules from the decision engine's output, with a written rationale for every token.

skills.name: accessible-form-design
skills.description: Design forms that are usable, forgiving, and accessible — clear labels, helpful inline validation, associated errors, logical focus order, and a recoverable submit.

skills.name: visual-hierarchy
skills.description: Build a clear visual hierarchy so users find what matters at a glance — using size, weight, colour, and spacing to encode priority rather than decoration.

skills.name: layout-foundations
skills.description: Compose layouts on a grid with intentional alignment and rhythm, so structure reads clearly and content has room to breathe across breakpoints.

skills.name: navigation-patterns
skills.description: Choose and structure navigation to match how often users switch contexts — sidebar for many frequently-visited sections, top nav for few, with clear current-location cues.

skills.name: design-principles
skills.description: The foundational design principles an agent should apply to every interface — hierarchy reflects priority, clarity beats decoration, and consistency reduces load. Start here before any UI work.

skills.name: responsive-design
skills.description: Design layouts that adapt across mobile, tablet, and desktop by prioritising content and using progressive disclosure — never by cramming a desktop layout onto a phone.

skills.name: spacing-system
skills.description: Use a consistent spacing scale and let layout containers own the gaps, producing even rhythm and grouping that communicates structure.

skills.name: empty-states
skills.description: Turn empty screens into onboarding moments — explain what belongs here and give a clear first action, distinguishing "nothing yet" from "no results" and "error".

skills.name: error-states
skills.description: Handle errors with recovery in mind — say what went wrong in plain language, why, and how to fix it, at the right level, without blame or dead ends.

skills.name: loading-states
skills.description: Communicate progress honestly during waits — skeletons for structured content, spinners for short indeterminate waits — while preserving layout and avoiding jank.

skills.name: typography-system
skills.description: Establish a type system — a modular scale, deliberate weights, and readable measures — that carries hierarchy and personality without relying on decoration.

knowledge.name: A modal for every interaction
knowledge.summary: Reaching for a modal dialog as the default container traps focus, breaks flow, stacks poorly, and fails on small screens.

knowledge.name: Use an editorial visual language when emotion and brand lead
knowledge.summary: When the product's job is to make people feel and trust — luxury, lifestyle, storytelling — favour editorial layout over utilitarian density.

knowledge.name: Use sidebar navigation for frequent section switching
knowledge.summary: When users move often between many top-level areas, a persistent sidebar keeps destinations visible and switching cheap; a top nav suits few destinations.

knowledge.name: The primary action is visually distinct
knowledge.summary: On any view, the single most important action should be immediately distinguishable from secondary and tertiary actions.

knowledge.name: Inline form validation
knowledge.summary: Validate and give feedback close to each field at the right moment, so users fix problems as they go rather than at a wall of errors on submit.

knowledge.name: Progressive disclosure
knowledge.summary: Show the few things most users need now; reveal advanced or secondary detail on demand, so complexity is available without being imposed.

knowledge.name: Clarity beats decoration
knowledge.summary: Visual effects must earn their place by improving comprehension; ornament that adds cognitive load without meaning is a cost, not a feature.

knowledge.name: Consistency reduces cognitive load
knowledge.summary: Reusing the same patterns, spacing, and language across an interface lets users transfer what they learned once to everywhere else.

knowledge.name: Visual hierarchy should reflect user priority
knowledge.summary: The most important thing for the user should be the most prominent thing on the screen; visual weight is a budget spent on what matters most.

knowledge.name: Text contrast meets WCAG AA
knowledge.summary: Body text needs a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background (3:1 for large text and meaningful UI components), in every theme.

knowledge.name: Every form control has a programmatic label
knowledge.summary: Inputs, selects, and textareas must have an associated label that assistive technology can announce — placeholder text is not a label.

knowledge.name: Every interactive element has a visible focus state
knowledge.summary: Anything a keyboard can reach must show a clearly visible focus indicator, so keyboard and switch users can always see where they are.

knowledge.name: Touch targets meet a minimum size
knowledge.summary: Tappable controls should be large enough and spaced enough to hit reliably — a common floor is 24x24 CSS px minimum, with 44x44 preferred for primary touch actions.
